data.title: Conservative justices reluctant to credit Black Codes in Hawaii gun law case
data.description:
Black Codes, enacted after the Civil War to restrict newly freed African Americans, took center stage at the Supreme Court Tuesday as it considered a challenge to a Hawaii gun law. 

With the conservative majority’s Second Amendment test requiring states to justify gun measures with historical analogues, Hawaii and other states have turned to the Black Codes to justify gun control efforts. 

At Tuesday’s arguments, Justice Neil Gorsuch and other conservative justices appeared reluctant to credit them given their racist origins. 

“I want to understand how you think Black Codes should inform this Court’s decision-making. It’s quite an astonishing claim to me,” Justice Neil Gorsuch asked Hawaii’s lawyer. 

“So, the Black Codes are undoubtedly a shameful part of our history,” responded the lawyer, Neal Katyal. “But that doesn’t at all mean that this particular law is irrelevant to Second Amendment analysis.” 

The Supreme Court heard arguments Tuesday on whether Hawaii can ban carrying a handgun on private property without the owner’s consent. Several Democratic-led states have similar measures. 

Three firearms owners and a local gun rights group, who are backed by the Trump administration, are challenging Hawaii’s law. They contend it runs afoul of the Supreme Court’s expanded gun rights test, which was announced in 2022 and requires gun control measures be consistent with the nation’s historical tradition of firearm regulation. 

Hawaii points to anti-poaching laws enacted near the nation’s founding and gun restrictions Louisiana passed in 1865 as part of its Black Codes.  

“They wanted to disarm the Black population in order to help the Klan terrorize them and law enforcement officers in that period in that region. They wanted to put them at the mercy of racist law enforcement officers,” said Justice Samuel Alito. 

“So is it not the height of irony,” he asked Katyal, “to cite a law that was enacted for exactly the purpose of preventing someone from exercising the Second Amendment right, to cite this as an example of what the Second Amendments protects.” 

Katyal said he agreed that parts of the Black Codes did exactly that. 

“Our point to you is this consent requirement did not operate that way,” Katyal added. 

The case marks one of the first Second Amendment battles in which the justices are set to apply their new gun rights test. A decision is expected by the summer. 

In 2024, the court ruled the test allows the government to disarm individuals with domestic violence restraining orders. 

On Tuesday, Alito was one of several conservative justices who appeared open to the challengers’ arguments.  

“Part of, again, what our precedents talk about in this area is that the Second Amendment has been treated as a sort of a second-level right,” Chief Justice John Roberts told Katyal. 

“When you’re looking for a historical tradition that justifies an exception to the textually expressed right, it has got to be a deeply rooted tradition, broadly consistent over time and broad among a lot of states,” said Justice Brett Kavanaugh. 

“You don’t have anything like that here. So just kind of, from your perspective, it’s pretty simple,” he told the challengers. 

The court’s liberal justices, however, appeared more skeptical of the argument. 

Justice Elena Kagan suggested at points that “pretty good evidence” of historical analogies exist for Hawaii’s law, noting the anti-poaching laws. 

“You say they’re not close enough, and I guess I want to know why,” Kagan said. “I mean, I was struck by the fact that there are quite a number of statutes that do exactly what this law does.” 

Near the start of the argument, Justice Sonia Sotomayor asked the challengers’ attorney directly if there is a constitutional right to carry a gun on private property. 

“Yes, justice,” responded Alan Beck, the attorney. 

Sotomayor replied, “I’ve never seen that right.”

data.title: Rollins: Viral meal costs remark meant to describe 'more robust plate'
data.description:
Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins said Tuesday that comments she made last week suggesting Americans could get a nutritious meal consisting of a piece of chicken, a piece of broccoli, a corn tortilla, and “one other thing” for just $3 were meant to describe a “more robust plate.”

Rollins clarified her remarks after being asked by a reporter whether she was being “a little bit flip” about the cost of groceries.

“No, and well, I regret that I didn’t make it sound like a more robust plate because that is what I mean to make it sound like,” Rollins told reporters outside the White House.

“A really big piece of chicken. And when I said a piece of broccoli, I meant like big. … I’m a mom of four, and I cook broccoli a lot. For me, that’s a big head of broccoli, a baked potato, etc., a couple pieces of bread,” she said.

Rollins’s remark on meal prices came during an interview on NewsNation last week, where she addressed concerns over whether the White House’s new dietary guidelines meant it would become more expensive to maintain a healthy diet.

“We’ve run over 1,000 simulations,” Rollins told anchor Connell McShane last Wednesday, insisting the Trump administration was not asking people to spend more on their diet.

“It can cost around $3 a meal for a piece of chicken, a piece of broccoli, you know, a corn tortilla and one other thing,” she continued. “So, there is a way to do this that actually will save the average American consumer money.”

Democrats immediately seized on a viral clip of her comments, ripping the administration for being out of touch.

An AI-generated image depicting a cafeteria tray with a piece of chicken, a piece of broccoli, a tortilla and a tinfoil package labeled “mystery item?” was posted to social media by Democrats on the House Ways and Means Committee, alongside a caption that said “MAHA!”

“The Trump administration is completely out of touch with the challenges families are facing,” Sen. Ed Markey (D-Mass.) said in another post. “They have no idea how much dinner costs, no interest in actually making groceries affordable, and no clue how to make ends meet.”

Rollins said Tuesday that she “sort of cringed” at the backlash and praised the administration’s efforts to lower food prices.

“I grew up with a single mom in a really small town and certainly never meant to be flippant, but I also think that the left or those that were attacking me perhaps know that this is an issue that 90 percent of Americans agree on,” Rollins said.

She added, “It is one that no one has really done anything about until this administration, and it is one where we will fundamentally, I believe, change the trajectory of our country by changing the health of our country, and I’m really proud of that.”

The updated guidelines rolled out by Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. earlier this month invert the food pyramid, placing protein, healthy fats, dairy, fruits and vegetables at the top and whole grains at the bottom.

“For decades, the Dietary Guidelines favored corporate interests over common-sense, science-driven advice to improve the health of Americans. That ends today,” HHS stated in a post on the social platform X. “ The new Dietary Guidelines call for prioritizing high-quality protein, healthy fats, fruits, vegetables, and whole grains—and avoiding highly processed foods and refined carbohydrates.”

Rollins said a day’s worth of meals under the new guidelines — three full square meals and a snack — currently costs about $15.64, citing a U.S. Department of Agriculture economist.

data.title: Maine hits pause on undercover license plates for federal immigration officers
data.description:
Officials in Maine on Tuesday said they would halt federal immigration officers from being granted undercover license plates as reports of statewide enforcement operations surface.

“The Maine Bureau of Motor Vehicles received a request for confidential, undercover Maine license plates from U.S. Customs and Border Protection. These requests in light of rumors of ICE deployment to Maine and abuses of power in Minnesota and elsewhere raise concerns,” Maine Secretary of State Shenna Bellows said in a statement. 

“We have not revoked existing plates but have paused issuance of new plates. We want to be assured that Maine plates will not be used for lawless purposes,” she added.

Maine is a sanctuary state, meaning it limits local authorities’ compliance with federal immigration enforcement efforts.

Maine Gov. Janet Mills (D) has strongly encouraged residents to stay vigilant amid the surge in immigration enforcement activity launched by the Trump administration. 

“To the federal government I say this: If your plan is to come here to be provocative and to undermine the civil rights of Maine residents, do not be confused — those tactics are not welcome here,” Mills said in a video statement last week. 

“To the people of Maine: I know you care deeply about protecting your neighbors, as do I. And I know that many of you are angry about the potential for any enhanced federal law enforcement presence in Maine. I feel that too,” she added.

The Democratic governor encouraged residents to meet hostility with “reserve and resolve.”

Her push for safety comes as Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers are expected to arrive in Portland, Maine, and Lewiston, Maine, in the coming days. Mills words follow the death of Minnesota mother Renee Good, who was killed by an ICE officer. Agents told her to step out of the vehicle, but she instead tried to drive away, and an officer fired shots into the car.

The Trump administration has described her as a “domestic terrorist” who used her vehicle as a “deadly weapon” to evade law enforcement.

data.title: 'Make America Go Away' caps gaining popularity amid Trump Greenland takeover talk
data.description:
Red hats sporting the words “Make America Go Away” have gained popularity among critics opposed to the U.S.’s push to take over Greenland.

The accessories are a parody of President Trump’s distinct red campaign hats that displayed his slogan “Make America Great Again.” A Danish company called Greenland Support is behind this viral phenomenon. 

“Greenland needs our support now, more than ever,” a statement on the company’s website reads. “Support the site, to support the Greenlandic people.”

In addition to the new “MAGA” hats, the company sells headwear with phrases like “Not for sale!” and “Nu det Nuuk!,” which translates to “Now it’s enough.”

The Trump administration is continuing to ramp up its threats to acquire the territory. Last Saturday, the president announced plans to impose tariffs on Denmark and other European countries who opposed the takeover the Arctic island — a semi-autonomous Danish territory.

The import tax on goods from these countries begins at 10 percent on Feb. 1 and will increase to 25 percent on June 1 “until such time as a Deal is reached for the Complete and Total purchase of Greenland,” the president wrote in a lengthy Truth Social post.  

Danish officials have condemned these penalties, with Danish Chamber of Commerce CEO Brian Mikkelsen writing online that, “Trump’s farce continues.” 

“The American president is once again using tariffs as a threat,” Mikkelsen wrote on social platform X in a translated post. “As so many times before, it is damaging to confidence in world trade and damaging to the American and European economies.”

The U.S.’s pursuit of Greenland is widely unpopular among Americans, according to a Quinnipiac University poll released last week. 

The survey, which compiled responses from over 1,000 registered American voters, found that 86 percent of respondents disapprove of the U.S. utilizing military force to take over Greenland.

Additionally, 55 percent disapprove of an attempt to purchase the territory, while 37 percent were in favor, according to the poll.

data.title: Attorney General Bondi wants a letter of support from me. No way
data.description:
Attorney General Pam Bondi is reportedly hitting a rough spot with President Trump. So, Department of Justice employees have started reaching out to various conservative organizations seeking praise and support. One Department of Justice staffer reached out to me.

It’s not going to happen.

I received an email recently thanking the free market think tank I had been associated with for many years — but I haven’t been there for nearly two years. It invited the organization “to submit a letter of support recognizing the Attorney General’s work.” I can’t speak for the organization, but I can speak for me.

The letter cites several of what Department of Justice calls Bondi’s achievements. I agree with some. But the one that really caught my attention was the first one on the list: “Under the Attorney General’s leadership, the Department has made significant progress in restoring impartial justice by eliminating politicized prosecutions.”

To the contrary, “politicized prosecutions” have come to define this Department of Justice.

Start with the firing of Justice Department attorneys who pushed back against convicting people on Trump’s enemies list.

Last September, Erik Siebert, a U.S. attorney for the Eastern District of Virginia, resigned because he couldn’t find enough evidence to charge New York Attorney General Letitia James, a Democrat, with mortgage fraud. After the resignation, Trump told the press, “He didn’t quit, I fired him!”

And recently, the department fired Robert McBride, the number-two official in the Eastern District of Virginia, for requesting not to lead the prosecution of former FBI Director James Comey. McBride reportedly thought it would be too difficult to prosecute the case and run the department’s office.

The politicization has become blatantly apparent even to the public. Remember Trump’s Truth Social text to Bondi complaining: “We can’t delay any longer, it’s killing our reputation and credibility. They impeached me twice, and indicted me (5 times!), OVER NOTHING. JUSTICE MUST BE SERVED, NOW!!!” Both James and Comey were soon indicted, but a judge has since dismissed the charges.

Trump is not happy with how long his political retribution is taking. Dozens of federal prosecutors from around the country recently came to the White House for a photo shoot. The president reportedly turned it into a grievance platform. “Trump criticized them as ineffective, saying the group was making it difficult for Bondi and Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche to do their job,” according to the Wall Street Journal. One of Trump’s complaints is that Justice hadn’t yet brought a case against Sen. Adam Schiff (D-Calif.), a prominent Trump critic.  

Trump’s gripe must have gotten the attention of Jeanine Pirro, the U.S. attorney for the District of Columbia. The day after the meeting, her office sent grand jury subpoenas to the Federal Reserve Bank, initiating a criminal investigation into Fed Chair Jerome Powell. A firestorm of negative reactions ensued.

Another problem is that after firing so many qualified attorneys, the DOJ has had difficulty filling the vacancies, according to the American Bar Association. 

Trump’s demand for quick retribution has meant ignoring the legal process for filling some top vacancies, forcing the courts to step in. For example, Politico reports, “So far, courts have sided against [Trump’s] attempts to circumvent the Senate confirmation process and disqualified prosecutors in Nevada, the Los Angeles area, Virginia and elsewhere.” Add New Jersey and New York to that list.

Don’t get me wrong — Democrats ramped up and even boasted about launching politically motivated prosecutions against Trump. Ironically, many people think those prosecutions helped Trump win the election, which might be his best revenge. But could Bondi’s political prosecutions now help Democrats in the midterm elections, or in 2028?

But it’s not just firing Justice Department attorneys for failing to indict — Bondi is also quashing active investigations. For example, the Department of Justice demanded federal attorneys drop charges of bribery and wire fraud against New York Mayor Eric Adams, a Democrat, once Adams got on board with Trump. Eight federal prosecutors for the Southern District of New York resigned as a result. Three of them penned and sent a resignation letter, writing, “Now, the department has decided that obedience supersedes all else, requiring us to abdicate our legal and ethical obligations in favor of directions from Washington.”

Speaking of “ethical obligations,” did I mention that Bondi fired her department’s top ethics official, Joseph Tirrell?

In another case, Trump’s future Border czar Tom Homan allegedly accepted $50,000 for influence peddling in an FBI undercover operation. That investigation was dropped. I could cite more examples, but you get the point.

The role of attorney general is complicated since it is both legal and political. But responsible attorneys general sometimes take positions the president doesn’t like. Recall that Trump’s first attorney general, Jeff Sessions, recused himself from the Russia investigation, which eventually got him fired. And Attorney General Bill Barr told Trump the department could not find evidence of widespread fraud in the 2020 presidential election. Barr was fired immediately.

Bondi’s problem is that there seems to be nothing she won’t do to please Trump. That’s going to leave a terrible legacy for her and the country. The Bondi era will likely become its own chapter in the history books and law school textbooks about the dangers of politicized prosecutions at the Department of Justice.

Merrill Matthews is the Texas state chair of Our Republican Legacy.

data.title: Live updates: Trump doubles down on Greenland threats; White House to brief press
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday is doubling down on his threats to seize Greenland, as European allies push back on the president.

Overnight, Trump released private messages between himself and Emmanuel Macron, in which the French president questioned Trump’s end game with Greenland.

“My friend, we are totally in line on Syria, we can do great things on Iran. I do not understand what you are doing on Greenland,” Macron wrote, per a screenshot shared on Truth Social.

Later on Tuesday, Trump will travel to Davos in Switzerland for the World Economic Forum, where he will meet with several of these allies amid his mounting pressure.

Back in Washington, the Supreme Court released opinions and heard oral arguments on a case involving gun restrictions in Hawaii.

The Senate is in recess, while House members make their way back to Washington as a Jan. 31 shutdown deadline looms. Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.), meanwhile, is in London, where he spoke to the U.K. Parliament. He is the first sitting speaker to do so.

Tensions in Minnesota are still high, as the Trump administration has boosted federal law enforcement’s presence in the state amid massive protests following two Immigration and Customs Enforcement officer-involved shootings in Minneapolis.

Before departing for Davos, Trump will participate in a “signing time” at 3 p.m. Tuesday also marks one full year of Trump’s second term, and White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt will hold a 1 p.m. press briefing.

Follow live below.

data.title: Tillis 'not going to go to impeachment' over Greenland
data.description:
Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) on Tuesday said he would not back an impeachment effort against President Trump over the administration’s push to acquire Greenland.

“I’m not going to go to impeachment. Let’s say it was a kinetic action — I’d immediately go for a War Powers Resolution,” Tillis said during an appearance on CNBC.

The comment comes after the Senate failed to pass a War Powers Resolution that sought to block the U.S. military from continuing its use of force in Venezuela following the capture and ouster of Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro.

A bipartisan group of lawmakers have aired grievances over Trump’s quips about acquiring the semi-autonomous Danish territory, but few have signaled impeachment. 

Rep. Don Bacon (R-Neb.), who is retiring at the end of his term, said he’d consider the idea of impeaching the president over his foreign policy measures.

“It’s ridiculous that this has to even be done,” Bacon told the Omaha World-Herald. “But when the president talks about taking Greenland one way or the other way every day this last week or so and that it’s unacceptable if Greenland refuses to be part of the United States, I felt like I needed to make a statement that Republicans disagree.”

Sen. Mitch McConnell (Ky.) warned in a speech on the Senate floor last week that seizing Greenland could “incinerate” the nation’s long-standing ties with NATO allies.

“Unless and until the president can demonstrate otherwise, then the proposition at hand today is very straightforward: incinerating the hard-won trust of loyal allies in exchange for no meaningful change in U.S. access to the Arctic,” McConnell said, delivering one of the strongest statements criticizing the Trump administration’s talk about potentially seizing the Arctic island by force.

The Kentucky Republican also suggested the president was being “ill-advised,” as did Bacon. 

The U.S. currently has military access to Greenland through the Pituffik Space Base, which lawmakers have suggested utilizing in place of annexing the island.

Democrats have strongly opposed the move to takeover Greenland with other implications regarding the Trump administration’s endeavors overseas — from Venezuela to strikes on alleged drug boats in the Caribbean to potential moves on Iran — since the start of his second term.

Trump told GOP lawmakers earlier this month that if Democrats win big in the midterm elections, he’s sure to face impeachment. During his first term, he became the first president to be impeached twice.

data.title: Read Trump's texts with European leaders over Greenland
data.description:
President Trump has shared messages from European leaders over his mounting pressure campaign to take control of Greenland, as he heads to the Davos economic forum on Tuesday night.

On Monday, reports emerged that Trump sent a message to Norway’s prime minister, Jonas Gahr Støre, suggesting his failure to win the Nobel Peace Prize was a factor in his threats against Greenland. 

Støre’s office later said that Trump shared the message with other NATO leaders, and released a message from Støre and Finnish President Alexander Stubb that promoted Trump’s response.

Text message from Støre to Trump Jan. 18:

Dear Mr President, dear Donald – on the contact across the Atlantic – on Greenland, Gaza, Ukraine – and your tariff announcement yesterday. You know our position on these issues. But we believe we all should work to take this down and de-escalate – so much is happening around us where we need to stand together. We are proposing a call with you later today – with both of us or separately – give us a hint of what you prefer! Best – Alex and Jonas

Text message Trump to Støre about 30 minutes later: 

Dear Jonas: Considering your Country decided not to give me the Nobel Peace Prize for having stopped 8 Wars PLUS, I no longer feel an obligation to think purely of Peace, although it will always be predominant, but can now think about what is good and proper for the United States of America. Denmark cannot protect that land from Russia or China, and why do they have a ‘right of ownership’ anyway? There are no written documents, it’s only that a boat landed there hundreds of years ago, but we had boats landing there, also. I have done more for NATO than any other person since its founding, and now, NATO should do something for the United States. The World is not secure unless we have Complete and Total Control of Greenland. Thank you! President DJT

On Tuesday morning, Trump shared incoming messages from NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte and French President Emmanuel Macron on Truth Social. NATO and French officials confirmed their authenticity to The Hill. 

Undated message from Rutte to Trump: 

Mr. President, dear Donald – what you accomplished in Syria today is incredible. I will use my media engagements in Davos to highlight your work there, in Gaza, and in Ukraine. I am committed to finding a way forward on Greenland. Can’t wait to see you. Yours, Mark

The message apparently references a ceasefire deal struck Sunday between Syria’s government and the Kurdish-led Syrian Democratic Forces. There are already reports of renewed clashes in the country’s north. 

Undated message from Macron to Trump:

My friend,
We are totally in line on Syria
We can do great things on Iran
I do not understand what you are doing on Greenland
Let us try to build great things :

  1. i can set up a g7 meeting after Davos in Paris on thursday afternoon. I can invite the ukrainians, the danish, the syrians and the russians in the margins
  2. let us have a dinner together in Paris together on thursday before you go back to the us

Emmanuel

Trump did not include his response to the message or any ensuing messages from Rutte or Macron. 

Speaking to reporters on Monday night, Trump was asked about Macron’s resistance to joining the “Board of Peace,” a body headed by Trump with ambitions to implement a peace plan in Gaza and potentially elsewhere. 

Trump said Macron would soon be out of office, adding, “I’ll put a 200 percent tariff on his wines and Champagnes, and he’ll join, but he doesn’t have to join.”

Macron delivered a scathing speech at Davos on Tuesday warning the U.S. wants to “weaken and subordinate” Europe.

data.title: Rep. Steve Womack announces wife's death
data.description:
Rep. Steve Womack is mourning the death of his 68-year-old wife, Terri, after more than four decades of marriage.

The Arkansas Republican said Tuesday that Terri Lynn Williams Womack died Sunday following a brief illness. 

“Heaven welcomed an angel,” Womack wrote in a social media post, saying his wife “went to live in eternity with Jesus.”

“My heart is broken and that space remains without her presence,” Womack said of his spouse, who spent her career working for various courts in Arkansas.

Thanking the public for the “outpouring of prayers and support,” Womack said, “I take comfort in knowing she is no longer suffering in pain and is at peace evermore.”

Described as an “avid landscaper” and an “excellent cook” who adored her pets, an obituary for Terri said that her three children and four grandchildren were “most important to her.” 

“With all boys, she was confined to learning and participating in the rough and tumble activities of those kids,” the obituary said. 

Terri Womack died just days after President Trump granted clemency to the congressman’s son, James Phillip Womack, after he was convicted in a federal drug case.

According to USA Today, a White House official had noted following the commutation that the Womacks had “several recent health issues, including his mother being diagnosed with abdominal cancer.”

data.title: Replica of alleged Trump Epstein birthday note appears on National Mall
data.description:
A large replica of a birthday note attributed to President Trump that was reportedly included in a book given to Jeffrey Epstein for his 50th birthday in 2003 appeared on the National Mall in Washington D.C. on Monday, a day before what would’ve been the convicted sex offender’s 73rd birthday.

A plaque inviting visitors to sign the card with a message to the Trump administration sits next to a box of Sharpies in front of the card. It asks people to refrain from “promotional, violent, or hateful speech” and notes such rhetoric would be removed.

The 10-foot-tall replica was erected by The Secret Handshake, according to multiple news outlets, an anonymous group responsible for several other statues depicting Trump and Epstein that have popped up at the same location in recent months, including one of the pair holding hands.  

“Kudos to these Trump Deranged Liberals for constantly inventing new ways to light Democrat donor money on fire by spreading fake news,” White House spokesperson Abigail Jackson said in a statement about the new installation. “When will they put up the statue about Democrats like Stacey Plaskett and Hakeem Jeffries who continued to solicit money and meetings from Epstein after he was a convicted sex offender.”

Trump has repeatedly denied writing the note, which was released by Democrats on the House Oversight Committee last September.

It shows the outline of a naked woman’s figure with a message in the middle that appears to be a scripted conversation between Trump and Epstein, referencing “certain things in common” between the two.

“A pal is a wonderful thing. Happy Birthday — may every day be another wonderful secret,” it reads.

Trump sued The Wall Street Journal, the first outlet to report about the alleged letter, earlier last summer. The White House has maintained that the president’s signature was a forgery.

Trump’s Department of Justice (DOJ) has faced growing pressure over its handling of files related to Epstein, with a new CNN poll finding that only 6 percent of Americans were satisfied with the amount of information that’s been released.

The poll was conducted about a month after the DOJ’s congressionally mandated December deadline to release all unclassified documents concerning Epstein and his longtime associate Ghislaine Maxwell, with redactions to protect the victims.

The same survey found that two-thirds of respondents said they thought the federal government was intentionally holding back some information that should be released.

The DOJ said in an early January court filing that it had released approximately 12,285 documents in accordance with the Epstein Transparency Act and was in the process of reviewing over 2 million more files.
